audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Festus, Jerusalem transfer request, Caesar appeal, Agrippa/Bernice, Festus summary.

verses.restored: Festus therefore, entering the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Caesarea.
The chief priests and first ones of the Judeans informed him against Paul, and they were urging him,
asking a favor against him, that he would summon him to Jerusalem, making an ambush to kill him along the way.
Festus answered that Paul was being kept in Caesarea, and that he himself was about to go there quickly.
Therefore, he said, let those powerful among you go down with me; if there is anything out of place in the man, let them accuse him.
Spending among them not more than eight or ten days, he went down to Caesarea. The next day, sitting on the judgment seat, he commanded Paul to be brought.
When he arrived, the Judeans who had come down from Jerusalem stood around him, bringing many heavy charges that they were not able to prove.
Paul defended himself: Neither against the Law of the Judeans, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have I sinned in anything.
Festus, wishing to lay up favor with the Judeans, answered Paul and said, Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and be judged there before me concerning these things?
Paul said, I stand before Caesar's judgment seat, where it is necessary for me to be judged. I have wronged the Judeans in nothing, as you also know very well.

If therefore I am doing wrong and have done anything worthy of death, I do not refuse to die. But if there is nothing to the things these accuse me of, no one is able to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar.
Then Festus, having conferred with the council, answered, To Caesar you have appealed; to Caesar you shall go.
After some days had passed, Agrippa the king and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to greet Festus.
As they were spending many days there, Festus set Paul's case before the king, saying, There is a certain man left prisoner by Felix.
When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and elders of the Judeans informed me about him, asking for a sentence against him.
I answered them that it is not the custom of Romans to hand over any human before the accused has the accusers face to face and receives opportunity for defense concerning the charge.
Therefore, when they came together here, I made no delay; the next day, sitting on the judgment seat, I commanded the man to be brought.
The accusers stood around him, bringing no charge of evil things such as I suspected,
but they had certain questions against him concerning their own religion and concerning a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul was claiming to live.
Being perplexed about inquiry concerning these things, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem and be judged there concerning these things.
But Paul, appealing to be kept for the decision of the Emperor, I commanded him to be kept until I send him to Caesar.
Agrippa said to Festus, I myself was wishing to hear the man. Tomorrow, he says, you shall hear him.
Therefore on the next day, when Agrippa and Bernice came with much display and entered the audience hall with commanders and prominent men of the city, and Festus commanded, Paul was brought.

Festus says, King Agrippa and all men present with us, you behold this one concerning whom the whole multitude of the Judeans petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, shouting that he ought not to live any longer.
But I found he had done nothing worthy of death. Since he himself appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.

I have nothing certain to write to the lord concerning him. Therefore I brought him before you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after examination happens, I may have something to write.
For it seems unreasonable to me, sending a prisoner, not also to indicate the charges against him.
